text: Fav tidbits from this: if you hit a 20 popularity score, you start getting an algorithmic push on release radar. If you hit about a 30, you start getting it on discover weekly as well. To get a popularity for a 20 on average about 3800 streams. And that 3,800 will get you a release radar push. You needed 2,500 before. So you need about 50% More Streams to get the same popularity index as before now. It’s gotten harder to hit to increase popularity scores, which makes it harder to get on algorithmic playlist. Market saturation could affect this too. The most popular song is the more streams you’re gonna get from Radio. The more you market, the more they’re gonna give you. The more popular you are, the more you get for free from them. But makes sense, because more popularity, the more data they have and the more they can recommend your music. Streams are the only things that matter. Saves, playlist adds, release date, etc- don’t really matter. They just benefit because they lead to more streams. Streams are a side effect of all the other things. It takes about 13,000 streams to get a discover weekly push.

text: Thank you for this, Andrew. We have made a similar analysis with 175k  songs we're extending it to 1.5mio songs, but it just needs time. However, one of the problems we have faced with the analysis, that there's one major driver that explains (r2) 95% of the predictiable success of a song, and that's this factor: major label and/or it's subsidiaries. When removing that factor our R-squared is close to 65%. So, honestly, I'm surprised you come up with 92% using only a fraction of samples to test with. Anyway, wishing you great success improving your model, curious to see and hear more!

text:
It really varies, tbh. Sometimes Release Radar drives way more engagement, sometimes Discover Weekly is better.

The cool thing about DW is that it's specifically geared to push your music out to new fans. In other words, it's a clear sign that Spotify thinks you're worth pushing.

But yeah, in terms of actual engagement, DW definitely functions as a badge of honor ha.
author: @twostorymediamusic
_video_title: How We Triggered Discover Weekly

text:
Hard to say, but hypothetically, yeah, you could get there twice as fast by doubling your spend (assuming streams doubled along with the spend).

There's a general rule of thumb that 10k streams w/ a 10% save rate will trigger DW, so the quicker you get to those numbers, the quicker, supposedly, you get to DW. But it's definitely not set in stone; I've seen those numbers fail to trigger DW, and I've seen smaller numbers succeed in triggering it.

Long-story-short: 🤷

text:
I have a giant playlist with wildly different genres that I regularly listen to and here’s some of the main observations I’ve made with it
1. Spotify prioritizes the most recently added songs to a playlist
2. Spotify will do its best to group songs with similar genres before moving onto another genre
3. Spotify will avoid songs that do not fit into the prominent genres of the playlist
4. It will prioritize genres that you have recently listened to. It’s also good to mention that it doesn’t count a song as listened to if you skip if before 30 seconds

So the public likes it dumb. We get it. Girls just wanna have fun. No news.

The crap dies. The good stuff is there, buried under tons of husks of counterfeit music. The shit will rot away, and the diamonds will shine through. It has always been so.

This decade will leave jewels like Behemoth, Mastodon, Enslaved, Animals as Leaders, Fleshgod Apocalypse, Arch Enemy and Eluveitie embedded in the coal, and eventually the wise men and women will uncover and treat those gems. text:
Tip for anyone who struggles with Apple Music song suggestions not being the best;  There is a way to fix that in Apple Music. I had that issue initially but I figured out how to fix it: go to settings, enable “use listening history”, and then any song you like make sure to heart/favorite/like it and then as you continue to use it, the personalized playlists like “(your Apple Music name)’s station”, then continue to like songs etc. then if some songs repeat on that station tap the station icon and it refreshes. it’s starts working really well when you do this

Edit/Correction (5/25): it is “Use Listening history” not “use offline line history”

Adding to this (date 5/25):
Most people when they go to stations that suggest for you individual and/or recommendations playlist. Some of them update at different times / time intervals (which it will show you how long ago it was updated).

text:
A lot of the stuff I listen to today is just made by the people i used to listen to 4-5 years ago.
Bit i bit i'll remember they exist and i check out if they have any new stuff, or i relisten to the stuff i already knew and sometimes form a completely different kind of bond with it.
If groups/ artists I like have collabed with others, i sometimes check those peoples stuff out as well.
Another cool way to find music I didn't know already is through following channels like Audiotree live, that's how I first found out about Petey and John Roseboro. Same goes for watching Tiny Desk performances, for example i first got into Sampha's music by watching his tiny desk.
Being in fandoms has also really helped me. I regularly get bored of a fandom after ive been in it for usually 3-4 months or so, but i adore all kinds of fanworks and usually just slurp up all the animatics i can get my hands on. I've found Shoe and Arthur (Shea) through the HLVRAI fandom, and Bill Callahan through the pathologic fandom.
Other than that, just sharing the music you like with other people can go a long way! Don't be afraid to ask for, or to give out recommendations to anyone who's interested :-)
(Last suggestion i have is buying physical music at thrift stores or record shops and the like, just picking out what you think looks cool, and with an open mind you can really find some awesome stuff that way)
